learning_outcomes.description: Identify and understand different dimensions of and approaches to peacebuilding.
Understand and explain some of the key ideas and assumptions associated with different approaches to peacebuilding.
Have some practical and theoretical appreciation of ethical and political dilemmas involved in peacebuilding.
Be aware of and able to articulate the challenges of working across cultures - conceptually and in concrete terms.
Synthesise, analyse and critically assess key concepts and arguments presented in the relevant literature.
Demonstrate, through research and communication, your understanding of the field.

assessments.task_description:
Reading the required texts in the course's reading list is essential for coming prepared to class and for success in POLS7505.

In each seminar in Weeks 2-7, you will be given 10 minutes to complete a short, handwritten, closed book quiz. The precise timing of the quiz during the seminar's 4-hour window may vary week-by-week.

Each quiz is worth 5 marks, divided in two parts. The first part will ask you to define a key concept of one of the readings (worth a maximum 2 pts). The second part will ask you to explain how this concept helps to understand or critically assess peacebuilding practice, based on the key points the author(s) made in the reading (worth a maximum 3 pts).

While there are 6 opportunities to take the quiz, only 4 will count towards your final mark, up to a maximum of 20%. Your top 4 results will comprise your mark if you take more than 4 quizzes. Since this allows you to miss up to two of the six quizzes, you will not be able to re-sit individual quizzes and no alternative assessment will be available. 

If you have a disability plan that prevents you from taking hand-written quizzes, please get in touch with the course's staff as soon as possible to find suitable arrangements. Special consideration for alternative assessment will only be given under highly unusual and well-documented circumstances, to students who are unable to attend more than one seminar during which quizzes are taking place. As there'll be only one opportunity to take an alternative quiz, please let the course coordinator know no later than 4 September and provide all the necessary documentation.

Please note:

This assessment task is to be completed in person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempt to use AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Students will be given 10 minutes to complete a hand-written, closed book quiz. Quizzes will be completed in-class and submitted immediately to the lecturer.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Peace processes are inherently complex and politically fraught, and even successful negotiations rarely translate into sustained and uncontested peace. The relative effectiveness of a peace proccess is shaped by how it was designed and implemented, who participated, and whether it was able to address the underlying drivers as well as legacies of armed conflict and mass violence.

Working in groups, students will produce a 4000 word evaluation report of a historical or contemporary peace process (e.g., Bougainville, Aceh, Myanmar, Mindanao, South Sudan, Colombia, Nepal, etc.) that assesses the conflict context, peace process, and peace agreement (where applicable), and critically evaluates peace implementation with respect to sustainable peacebuilding outcomes.

Your report should draw on relevant evidence and literature to explain and critically evaluate:

  • the drivers and dynamics of the conflict;
  • the objectives and design of the peace process;
  • the roles of relevant national, local, regional, and international actors;
  • how key issues such as security, governance, justice, reconciliation, power-sharing, and development were addressed;
  • challenges to implementation;
  • the extent to which the peace process has contributed to sustainable peace; and
  • the key lessons the case offers for future peacebuilding efforts in that or other contexts.

Please consult the marking criteria sheet in Blackboard for more information on expectations. Citation practice should conform to the standards set out in the School of Political Science and International Studies Essay Guide.

Please note:

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use Al and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative Al or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of Al and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using Turnitin.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
You will present the findings of your peace process evaluation to the class in a 15-minute group powerpoint presentation during our final seminar in Week 7. Your presentation will be followed by a Q&A, in which each group member will be asked to respond to a question by the course coordinator. Presentations will be recorded.

You should supplement your presentation with a 1-2 page 'explainer' handout for the class. The handout should succinctly communicate your group's key findings and analysis using a clear, accessible, and visually engaging format. The aim is to highlight the most important insights from your presentation rather than reproduce it in full.

This assignment is worth 20 marks: 10 are based on the quality of the group presentation; 10 are based the quality of your individual response to questions. The questions will be intended to assess your knowledge of the peace process, the key debates that informed your analysis, and the sources and academic literature you used. Your group may also be asked to respond to questions from the instructor and classmates about your use of AI.

You should upload your powerpoint presentation to TurnitIn by 5pm on the day of your presentation.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Please note that submissions have to be made via the designated Turnitin Link (Blackboard), using Word-encoding( .doc, or .docx extensions).
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
This assignment invites you to develop a 2500 word Peacebuilding Philosophy Statement that articulates and defends your own approach to peacebuilding by synthesising relevant theories, concepts, debates, and lessons we've examined throughout the course.

Task Description:

Your task is to develop a clear, critical, and evidence-informed statement of your peacebuiding philosophy and explain how it would inform your approach to professional practice. Your statement should address the following:

  • Analyse the contemporary peacebuilding landscape by identifying the key drivers of violent conflict and the principal challenges to building and sustaining peace. You may focus your analysis on a specific conflict context or examine broader cross-cutting challenges affecting peacebuilding globally.
  • Critically assess the role of peacebuilding practitioners in this context by evaluating the knowledge, skills, attributes and approaches required to respond effectively to these challenges.
  • Evaluate what constitutes good peacebuilding practice: critically assess the principles, practices, and approaches most appropriate to contemporary peacebuilding contexts;
  • Identify gaps, blind spots, or emerging challenges that may be limiting peacebuilding effectiveness (e.g. what should this course, the UQ PACS program, or the wider peacebuilding field be addressing that it currently isn't?);
  • Reflect on the constraints and ethical, political, and practical dilemmas that peacebuilding practitioners must navigate, including the tensions and trade-offs between competing objectives or priorities in complex conflict settings;
  • Based on this analysis, Articulate and defend your own philosophy of peacebuilding, explaining (1) how it has been informed by this course, relevant scholarship, your lived experience, or other formative influences, and (2) how it would guide your approach to peacebuilding practice or other professional contexts.

The aim of this assessment is to produce a critical and scholarly statement that demonstrates your ability to synthesise the course material and relevant literature into a coherent, theoretically grounded, and evidence-informed philosophy of peacebuilding. While you may choose to reflect on how your own identity, personal background, or positionality has shaped your perspective, this is not simply an autobiographical or personal reflection. Your arguments should be supported by relevant academic literature and demonstrate critical engagement with contemporary peacebuilding debates.

The citation practice in the Statement should conform to the standards set out in the School of Political Science and International Studies Essay Guide.

Please consult the criteria sheet in Blackboard for more information on expectations.

Please note: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use Al and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative Al or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of Al and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Please note that submissions have to be made via the designated Turnitin Link (Blackboard), using Word-encoding( .doc, or .docx extensions).
